T01.claim: I have to cut my arm off.
T01.shortExplanation: Ralston described the decision and method he used to escape after his arm was pinned by a boulder.
T01.fullTruth: After more than five days trapped in Bluejohn Canyon, he broke the bones in his forearm, amputated it with a multitool, and reached rescuers.
T01.context: The playable excerpt is centered on the exact spoken line: “I have to cut my arm off.”
T01.transcript: I have to cut my arm off.
T01.editorialBoundary: The verdict applies only to the displayed statement and the evidence cited for this case.
T01.lesson: An extraordinary account becomes credible when injuries, location, and rescue records independently support it.

T09.claim: All they had to do was test the gun.
T09.shortExplanation: Hinton spoke after spending about thirty years on Alabama's death row.
T09.fullTruth: Ballistics evidence used against him could not be matched to the crime, and prosecutors dismissed the case after the Supreme Court ordered a new trial.
T09.context: The playable excerpt is centered on the exact spoken line: “All they had to do was test the gun.”

T10.claim: I did not set out to be a hero. I set out that night simply to do my job.
T10.shortExplanation: Jewell described his actions and commitment after being cleared in the Olympic Park bombing investigation.
T10.fullTruth: He discovered the bomb, helped evacuate the area, was formally cleared, and the actual bomber later pleaded guilty.
T10.context: The playable excerpt is centered on the exact spoken line: “I did not set out to be a hero. I set out that night simply to do my job.”
T10.transcript: I did not set out to be a hero. I set out that night simply to do my job.
T10.editorialBoundary: The verdict applies only to the displayed statement and the evidence cited for this case.
T10.lesson: Public suspicion is not proof; later records may vindicate a person treated as a suspect.

T15.claim: The only thing I could put my hope and trust in is God.
T15.shortExplanation: Okene described how he remained calm and prayed while trapped underwater.
T15.fullTruth: He survived for roughly sixty hours in an air pocket inside the sunken tugboat Jascon-4 before divers found him alive.
T15.context: The playable excerpt is centered on the exact spoken line: “The only thing I could put my hope and trust in is God.”
T15.transcript: The only thing I could put my hope and trust in is God.
T15.editorialBoundary: The verdict applies only to the displayed statement and the evidence cited for this case.
T15.lesson: Rare survival claims are strongest when the rescue itself is independently recorded.

T20.shortExplanation: Packard clarified that the whale held him in its mouth rather than swallowing him into its stomach.
T20.fullTruth: A humpback whale engulfed Packard in its mouth off Cape Cod and released him alive shortly afterward.
T20.context: The playable excerpt is centered on the exact spoken line: “I wasn't swallowed, Jimmy.”
T20.editorialBoundary: The verdict applies only to the displayed statement and the evidence cited for this case.
T20.lesson: Precise wording matters: being inside an animal's mouth is not the same as being swallowed into its stomach.

T23.shortExplanation: Megee described his condition and travel before surviving for seventy-one days in remote Australian bushland.
T23.fullTruth: Station workers found him severely malnourished after more than two months alone in the Northern Territory outback.
T23.context: The playable excerpt is centered on the exact spoken line: “I was very lucky.”
T23.editorialBoundary: The verdict applies only to the displayed statement and the evidence cited for this case.
T23.lesson: Separate a documented rescue and survival period from details that remain debated.
